summary:
For a pediatric patient presenting with an obstructive kidney stone causing severe pain and visible blood due to ultrasound findings indicating hydronephrosis secondary to obstruction by such sizable calculus (>2-3mm), extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy should first consider as treatment modality because it offers less invasiveness compared alternatives like open surgery or percutaneous nephrolithotomy while providing reasonable success rates even if slightly above typical thresholds recommended clinically; however careful monitoring post-procedure will ensure no complications arise necessitating further intervention beyond initial therapy provided through ESWL procedure itself.
To address the presented condition effectively without undue risk posed either operationally during surgical procedures nor long-term health implications stemming from delayed definitive care, initiating therapeutic measures centered around utilizing Extracorporeal Shock Wave Lithotripsy appears most prudent at present stage based upon current diagnostic information available concerning location & dimensions specific to affected organ system involved herein described contextually within broader scope encompassed by overall medical evaluation conducted thus far involving young
